title: 広い allowWrite + 内側 denyWrite -> 外側は書けるが内側は deny 勝ち (allow x ng)。「deny勝ち」を「そもそも書けない」から分離

probes.prompt: Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: echo x > $HOME/lab-nest/f.txt — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives. If it is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the result.

Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: echo x > $HOME/lab-nest/sub/f.txt — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives. If it is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the result.

notes: Two probes isolate 'deny wins' from 'nothing is writable'. allowWrite:[~/lab-nest] + denyWrite:[~/lab-nest/sub]. w-nest-outer (~/lab-nest/f.txt) is inside the allow region and OUTSIDE the deny region -> allow x ok, proving the allowWrite really opened the tree. w-nest-inner (~/lab-nest/sub/f.txt) is inside the narrower deny region -> allow x ng, proving deny takes precedence over the broader allow. Without the outer probe you could not tell 'deny wins' from 'the allow never took effect'. The permissions.allow:[Bash(*)] that this case used to carry was dropped: sandbox already auto-allows Bash (cf S2-a/S4-a), so it was not a path rule and not needed. NOTE: whether an explicit re-allow INSIDE a deny region (allowWrite of ~/lab-nest/sub/inner) can re-open it is a separate stronger claim, not tested here (S2 GAPS G2 backlog).
hypothesis: In the sandbox FS layer deny wins over allow: with allowWrite:[~/lab-nest] the outer path is writable (allow x ok) but the carved-out denyWrite:[~/lab-nest/sub] blocks the inner path (allow x ng).
